observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
Consider the transition to a payroll payment system through bank Automated Teller Machine (ATM) cards instead of CA, to minimize the risks associated with handling large amounts of cash; and
Direct the City Treasurer to instruct the AOs to stop the practice of transferring CA to another employees and refrain from granting additional CA unless proper settlement/accounting was made on the previous CA.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to:
Reassess the dormant/inactive receivables and evaluate its collectability. Also, set-up an allowance for impairment to present the net realizable value in accordance with IPSAS 21; and
Document all the efforts exerted to collect/recover the dormant/inactive accounts and collate all available documents in case request for authority to write-off said accounts is warranted.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary JEV to adjust and correct the balances of affected receivable accounts due to misclassifications, and henceforth furnish this Office a copy of the approved JEV for further verification.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
Head of the DepEd, Pasay City, to submit the RIS and distribution list of the schools supplies and materials to the GSO;
GSO to prepare the SSMI and submit it, together with the RIS and distribution list to the City Accountant; and
City Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances of the affected accounts.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
Ensure the proper recording of remittances of taxes to reflect the correct amount of unremitted taxes per each type of taxes; and
Reconcile the records in order to identify the errors in the recording of remittances and effect adjustments, as necessary.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
Facilitate the verification and reconciliation of the amount of P1,374,618.08 to determine correct amount due for remittance, and remit immediately to the BIR the amount due, and religiously observe thereafter the remittance of withheld taxes within the prescribed period to avoid the imposition of penalties, interests, and surcharges by the BIR; and
Closely coordinate with the HRMD Office for the timely submission of payrolls to ensure the recognition of withheld taxes on the taxable period to which they relate.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
Ensure that corresponding withholding of loan repayments were recognized in the books of accounts before remittance is made to avoid over-remittance of loan repayments withheld; and
Reconcile the records to identify the errors in the recording of remittances and effect adjustments, as necessary.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor immediately and effectively implement the sports development program for the benefit of the intended youth beneficiaries.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to coordinate with the Source Agency for the execution of MOA regarding the cash prize on anti-illegal drugs campaign, otherwise refund the unutilized balance to the Source Agency.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to submit the quarterly reports to the Source Agency in accordance with the provisions of the MOA.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to reclassify the errors in the recording of the transactions.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to:
Strictly observe the compliance with the provisions of 2019 RIRR of RA No. 9514 regarding the use and recording of the Fire Safety Inspection Fees; and
Reclassify the disbursements charged against the Due to NGAs – BFP – Inspection Fees account to the Fire Code Fees – 20 percent share of LGU account and remit the same to the BFP.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Market Administration Office and the CTO–Market Division to strictly impose the collection of overdue accounts and the imposition of surcharges thereon from delinquent stallholders and to strictly comply with the provisions of the City Revenue Code on those stallholders who were not able to pay despite due notification.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor require the City Veterinary Office and CTO to:
Exert effort in collecting the outstanding inspection fees and charges for the year from the Slaughterhouses in accordance with its power to create sources of revenue; and
Review the provisions pertaining to the collections of inspection fees and charges and make necessary action to align with the rules and regulations of the City Revenue Code.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:
Submit immediately to the Audit Team the lacking documents in the DVs for verification and audit to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit; and
Stop immediately the practice of processing payments without complete supporting documents.

Henceforth, we recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor direct the City Planning and Development (CPD) Office, in coordination with the CEO, to continue monitor the completion of the seven projects and ensure optimal implementation of all planned socio-economic and environmental development projects funded out of the 20% DF.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct:
a.	The City Budget Officer to effect the reversion of the unexpended balances to the unappropriated surplus of the GF to be available through subsequent enactment thereafter, provide such copy to the Audit Team; and
b.	The CPD Office to coordinate with the Implementing Offices to evaluate and re-assess the unimplemented PPAs under the continuing appropriations whether still considered as priority projects of the City, otherwise, reprogram the fund.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor direct the:
CPD Officer and GFPS Chairperson to ensure the full implementation of GAD PPAs incorporated in the GPB in order to maximize the utilization rate of the GAD Fund; and
GFPS Chairperson to closely monitor the AIP’s alignment with the DILG-approved GPB, and ensure all changes to the GPB are submitted to the DILG Regional Office for review and approval.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor direct the CPD Office to adhere strictly to the submission deadlines outlined in COA Circular No. 2014-001 for future GPB and GAD AR submissions so that the timely review and evaluation can be undertaken.

We highly commended the effort of the City DRRM Officer for an efficient utilization of the 5% LDRRMF, however, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the OCA and the CTO to immediately transfer the actual cash intended for the DRRMF as of CY 2024 amounting to P142,946,064.43 and to strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 21 of RA 10121 and Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City DRRM Officer to reassess the feasibility of unimplemented PPAs under the Continuing Appropriations and rigorously monitor compliance with the provisions of Sections 11 (b) (1) and 12 (a) of RA No. 10121 or the Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Act of 2010.
We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to reclassify the PPA charged against the STF of LDRRMF for CY 2023 to the SGLF-IF Trust Fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to transfer the cash donations of P175,000.00 to the GF considering that the funds remain dormant for more than 10 years and the return of funds to the donors is no longer possible. It will serve as additional funds in providing services to its constituents.

We reiterated our prior year recommendations and the Management agreed that the City Mayor direct the CPD Office to intensify the review and assessment process to ensure that climate change PPAs are properly identified and tagged both in the AIP and CAIP to provide reliable, accurate and information to its intended users.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor require the CPD Officer to update its Local Climate Change Action Plan at least annually, in compliance with the Item 2.3.5 of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2014-135 dated October 21, 2014, to reflect changing social, economic, and environmental conditions and emerging issues.

We recommended that the City Mayor advise the:
City Accountant to:
Review the amount of P153,828,384.73 appearing as unremitted and verify from its records if there were remittances already made; and
Determine the exact amount of contributions unremitted, if there is any, and immediately remit the same.
City Accountant and the HRMD Officer to continue close coordination in the verification and reconciliation of the unremitted contributions as shown in the Notice of Default and the records of the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
HRMD Office to reconcile its records with the list of unpaid Emergency Loans based on the schedule given by the GSIS to determine the correct status of those Emergency Loan accounts; and
City Accountant and HRMD Office to closely coordinate in the determination of the exact amount of unremitted Emergency Loan repayments/ amortizations, if there is any, and cause the immediate remittance thereof.

We reiterated our recommendation in the prior year and the Management agreed that the City Mayor direct the Officer-in-Charge (OIC)-Head of PCGH to ensure strict compliance with the requirements outlined in Section 38, Article VIII of the RIRR of the NHIA of 2013, particularly in the proper preparation and submission of claims with complete and accurate documentation. Furthermore, strict adherence should be ensured to guarantee that claims are filed within the statutory 60-day period to prevent denied or RTH claims.

We also recommended that the OIC-Head of PCGH formulate and maintain a document tracking system to effectively monitor all claims/receivables transmitted to PhilHealth which contain information such as status of claim, whether paid, denied/RTH, reason thereof, refiled/appealed/complied and all material dates, for documentation and recording of transactions relating to PhilHealth claims.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

OCA to ensure that check disbursements are recorded based on the actual date of issuance of checks in compliance with Section 43, Chapter 3 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I;

OCT to prepare RCI based on the actual date of issuance/release of checks to claimants/payees; and

OCA and OCT to have proper coordination in monitoring checks which remain unreleased at year-end to ensure that appropriate adjustments could be made in a timely manner.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the OCA:
a. Ensure the timely recording of the transactions and remittance of correct amount of taxes withheld to avoid the surcharges, interests and penalties provided under Section 247 (b), Title X of the National Internal Revenue Code (NIRC) of 1997 and Revenue Memorandum Circular (RMC) No. 23-2007;
b. Coordinate with the HRMDO for the timely submission of payrolls for the computation of the exact amount of taxes withheld due for remittance; and
c. Ensure that corresponding taxes were recognized in the books of accounts before remittance is made to avoid over-remittance of taxes withheld; and
d. Reconcile the records in order to identify the errors in the recording of remittances and effect adjustments, as necessary.
observations.managementAction: Unimplemented The OCA ensures the timely recording of transactions and remittance of withheld taxes to avoid penalties and surcharges. The OCA coordinates with the HRMDO on the timely submission of payrolls. The OCA verifies the remittances made with the corresponding Subsidiary Ledgers to avoid over – remittances. The OCA reconciles the records to correct errors in recording remittances and effect the necessary adjustments.

observations.recommendation:

a. OCA to ensure that check disbursements are recorded based on the
actual date of issuance of checks in compliance with Section 43, Chapter
3 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I;
b. OCT to prepare RCI based on the actual date of issuance/release of
checks to claimants/payees; and
c. OCA and OCT to have proper coordination in monitoring checks which
remain unreleased at year-end to ensure that appropriate adjustments
could be made in a timely manner.

a. OCA to –
• Strictly enforce the liquidation of CAs granted to AOs within the
reglementary period;
• Send demand letters to all erring AOs who are still active in the
service and to submit their names to the CLO for appropriate action
including the immediate withholding of salaries and other unpaid
claims due to them;
b. OCLO to recommend to the City Mayor, the appropriate action to recover
the unliquidated CAs from the AOs who were already separated from the
service;
c. City Administrator to constantly remind concerned officials and
employees on the pertinent laws, rules and regulations governing the
handling of CA every time they are granted with; and
d. OCT to remind the SDO to submit immediately the liquidation report
including the official receipt as proof of refund of the unused CAs to
ensure the full liquidation.

a. Head of the PCGH to establish systematic procedures in the recording of
receipts and issuances of drugs and medicines and reconcile periodically
with the Billing department to determine the amount of drugs and
medicines which were administered, billed, and issued;
b. GSO and OCA to conduct thorough and periodic physical inventory of
drugs and medicines in the wards to ensure inclusion of unbilled drugs
and medicines in the RPCI and perform reconciliation between RPCI and
books of accounts for possible adjustment, if any; and
c. OCA to make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances of
the affected accounts.

4.18 We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

the affected accounts and provide the appropriate disclosures in the

• PCG’s future legal actions in consideration of the significant
subsequent events and circumstances related to the expropriated
land.

to:
a. Prepare the necessary journal entry to effect the adjustments in the
affected accounts; and
b. Derecognize unserviceable supplies and properties only upon
completion of the actual disposal and ensure that required supporting
documents are attached to the JEV.

unappropriated surplus the balances which have remained outstanding for

remittances and effect adjustments, as necessary.

26.6 We recommended that the City Mayor advise the LCPC to:

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor require the:
City Treasurer to submit the Real Property Tax Roll Register and used it as basis for the City Accountant to set up the RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year pursuant to Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGU, Volume I; and
City Accountant and the City Treasurer to reconcile the discrepancies between the RPT/SET Receivable accounts and the List of Real Property Tax Delinquencies, and prepare the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts pursuant to Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the City Mayor to require the:
GSO to prepare RPCI reflecting the existing balances as of year-end;
GSO and OCA to analyze and review the records of the inventory items with discrepancies, take the necessary action to resolve the discrepancies, and conduct regular reconciliation of their records to ensure correct balances of the inventory items at year-end pursuant to Section 491 of GAAM, Volume I.
GSO to ensure that the AIR and other supporting documents are immediately submitted to the OCA for timely recording of receipt of deliveries of inventory items pursuant to Section 121, Chapter 7 of MNGAS for LGU, Volume I; and
OCA to effect the necessary accounting entries to adjust the balances of the affected accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to:
Draw the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances of the affected accounts; and
Henceforth, record as part of Road Networks account only those capitalizable expenditures as provided under COA Circular 2015-008.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the General Service Officer to facilitate the undertaking of disposal of the unserviceable properties already supported with corresponding IIRUP in compliance with Section 162 of COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the GSO to, henceforth, file the application for disposal through the submission of the IIRUP to the CAO prior to the disposal of unserviceable properties pursuant to Sections 167 and 168, Chapter 7, Volume I of COA Circular No. 92-386, and Annex 51(A) of MNGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to:
Strictly observe and comply with the publicity requirements for government programs, projects and activities as required under COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013, to promote good governance through transparency and accountability and to encourage public participation in their implementation; and
Submit to the Audit Group at the beginning of the year the list of ongoing projects and projects to be implemented, in pursuant to Item 2.1 of the same Circular.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the OCA to analyze further the outstanding payables for more than two years and those without complete documentation to ascertain their validity. Otherwise, revert the accounts to the unappropriated surplus of the GF and SEF pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the OCE to:
Conduct verification of all on-going government PPAs based on the schedule provided under Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004; and
Regularly update the OCA on the status of accomplishments of on-going and completed government PPAs for fair presentation of related asset, liability, expense and equity accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:
The City Treasurer’s Office to remit the amount of P10,237,320.64 to the BTr and monitor funds due for remittance to national agencies such as the DPWH to facilitate and ensure that all shares due them are properly remitted; and
The OCA to ensure correct computation of the 5% DPWH share which shall be the basis of the CTO as to amount to be remitted to BTr.

We recommended that the City Mayor to require the City Treasurer’s Office to:
Transfer immediately the amount of P35,285,302.81 from GF to TF account; and
Ensure the fund transfer of the share of OBO.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Secure authorization from the DBM for the extension of implementation and utilization of financial assistance amounting to P14,548,309.00 since the project was already bidded and can no longer be cancelled; and
Direct the City Engineering Office to ensure timely implementation of the projects in order that funds are fully utilized.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Head of PSWD Office to ensure the:
Complete disposition of duties provided in every project proposal concerning the implementation of the socio-economic program of the City to attain more desirable output; and
Conduct of project monitoring, whenever required, in order to determine the effectiveness and sustainability of the implemented program or project which will be useful in decision making and evaluation of end-result.

We recommended that the City Mayor to:
Require the LFC and the Head of PSWD to prepare and post the required Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Program/Project Implementation on the City’s website and in three conspicuous public places in the locality within the set deadline in accordance with applicable DBM Local Budget Circulars that govern the issuance of LGSF and other Financial Assistance; and
Prepare and send the written notices to all the concerned oversight agencies of the National Government upon posting of the LGSF and other Financial Assistance Quarterly Reports on the City’s website.

We recommended that the City Mayor advise the:
City Administrator and Head, Pasay City Public Market to –
Explain the increase in Security and Personnel Services in CYs 2021 and 2020;
Submit report showing the consumption of electricity and water from Market Operations and of the stallholders, as basis for the City Accounting Office to record the correct amount of electricity and water expenses from Market Operations and in setting-up Other Receivables account which amount shall be equivalent to the consumptions of electricity and water by the stallholders;
City Accountant to –
Require the Head, PCPM to submit report showing the consumption of electricity and water from Market Operations and of the stallholders upon processing of payment of the electricity and water bills to Meralco and Maynilad, respectively; and
Record in the books of accounts the electricity and water consumption from Market Operations as expense and consumption of the stallholders as Other Receivables and credit the latter upon payment by the stallholders of the electricity and water bills to the City Treasurer’s Office.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to henceforth, record the payments advanced by the City to Maynilad and Meralco for the accounts of the stallholders as a debit to Other Receivables and a credit to Cash in Bank, and record the collections from the stallholders’ utility consumption as a debit to Cash in Bank and a credit to Other Receivables account.

We recommended that the City adopt strategies such as issuance of warrant of levy and institution of civil action against delinquent real property taxpayers to improve collection efficiency. Also, request assistance of the Barangays for the City’s tax campaign program, particularly in the dissemination of Notices of Delinquencies and serve warrants of levy to the delinquent real property owners within their territorial jurisdiction.

We recommended that the City Mayor advise the:
City Assessor to –
Review the Assessment Levels of 60% and 80% being imposed to residential and commercial condominium units, respectively, for condominium units with Market Values below P10,000,000.00 and consider the revisions;
Apply the Assessment Levels provided in Section 218 of the Act for Buildings and Other Structures to residential and commercial condominium buildings;
City Council to –
Consider the amendment of Section 5.b of the City Ordinance, in consonance with Section 218 of the Act; and
Study and consider increasing the unit values of condominium buildings if there is a need for the City Government to collect Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax equivalent to the amount when applying the 60% and 80% Assessment Levels.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the City Mayor require the:
City Planning and Development Officer and the City Budget Officer to fully utilize the 20% DF pursuant to DILG – DBM JMC 
No. 2017-01 and ensure that the implementation of all identified PPAs are given priority in order to warrant the full realization of the program and help achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the City;
City Planning and Development Officer, in coordination with the City Engineer, to monitor regularly the rate of progress of each project, and assure the completion of prior years’ unimplemented PPAs, as necessary; and
City Budget Officer and City Accountant to exert collaborative effort to review and reconcile their respective records in order to come up with the correct balance of obligations and actual cost incurred per PPAs.

We recommended that the City:
Continue its faithful observance of the budgetary requirements for GAD;
Establish a GAD M&E System for the effective and efficient implementation of GAD plans and programs thru the issuance of an addendum to Executive Order No. 48, s. 2021 for the effective monitoring of the GAD PPAs.
We further recommended that the City Mayor advise the:
GFPS to exert effort in mobilizing its proponent offices to accomplish their targeted and planned PPAs in the coming year; and
GFPS to include in the succeeding years the PPAs that are implementable in spite of the pandemic, which the end of it is still uncertain.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to effect the immediate Transfer from General Fund to Trust Fund the total amount of P193,557,225.95 to ensure that Trust Liabilities – DRRM account has sufficient cash and to stop the practice of simulated transfer of cash to ensure that the balances are recorded using the appropriate accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CSWMB to review and update the Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan of the City every two years or as the need arises in compliance with Section 4 (g), Rule VI of the IRR of RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the City Solid Waste Management Board and the Pasay City Environment and Natural Resources Office (PCENRO) in their continued efforts in complying with various environmental laws and policies, however, we still recommended that the City Mayor advise the Board and the PCENRO to comply with the requirements of the MBCRPP, particularly on the three activities/programs/criteria where it got zero scores to obtain higher overall rating in future assessment.

We recommended that the City Mayor advise the Head, PCENRO to require the ISWIMSI, to comply with the requirement to paint the information “Pasay City Keep our City Clean” and its Hotline Number for complaints relating to garbage to ensure that hauling services are intended exclusively for the City and such garbage trucks could not be used/assigned to service other cities in the NCR.

We recommended that the City Mayor to require the City Solid Waste Management Board thru the PCENRO, in coordination with the City Legal Office, to initiate the drafting of the Ordinance in prescribing the use and issuance of the ECT for violation of any existing sanitation and/or environmental protection ordinance and for other purposes.

We recommended that the City Mayor advise the City Social Development Officer to:
Identify and include PPAs which are doable under the present circumstances; and
Require and closely monitor the submission of the project proposals from the concerned offices to deter hindrances in the implementation of the lined up PPAs for the year.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the OCA to:
Ensure the timely recording of transactions and the full remittance of taxes withheld on due date to avoid possible imposition of surcharges, interests and penalties by the BIR.;
Coordinate with the Human Resource Management and Development Office (HRMDO) for the timely submission of payrolls to facilitate the recording of withholding of taxes in the books prior to the prescribed date of remittance; and
We further recommended the HRMDO to strictly require timely submission of DTRs for all departments and offices, particularly JOs, to facilitate indorsement of payrolls to OCA prior to the prescribed date of tax remittance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management continue and complete its investigation on the Cash - Local Treasury account balance of P988,241.26, without supporting cash on hand, and submit a report thereon for subsequent appropriate action.

We further recommended that the Office of the City Treasurer (OCT) and the OCA work together, to search for and examine all available records to identify the composition of the Cash - Local Treasury account and prepare adjusting entries, if warranted, duly supported with relevant documents based on the result of the analysis.

We recommended that the OCA:

Immediately draw journal entry vouchers to record all valid reconciling items that require adjustment in the books; and

Coordinate with the City’s depository banks for the prompt release of BS including the DM/CMs or other equivalent documents that would provide information on the nature of transactions affecting the bank account balances for recording in the books in the appropriate period.

We recommended that Management require the:
City Legal Office (CLO) to –
Revisit the Terms of the Loan, Additional Conditions and other pertinent provisions of the Loan Agreement with the PNB in order that amounts received from the national government, such as the IRA, may be deposited to the City’s AGDB account as required under DOF DC No. 01-2017;
Evaluate the guidelines issued by the BTr on the transfer of funds to the AGDB, in particular the required clearance from PNB, as provided under Section 2.2.2 of TC No. 01-2017, if still needed, with due consideration of the options mentioned in Section 3.01 (b) of the Loan Agreement to facilitate issuance of authority by the SP; and
Ensure strict compliance to the applicable provisions of the Loan Agreement with PNB despite changes to be made;
CTO to –
Immediately transfer the remaining deposits from PNB account no. 1510-xxxx03, to any of the AGDBs listed under Section 2 of the DOF DC No. 01-2017 after compliance with the requirements set with pertinent rules and regulations; and
Cause the closing of PNB account no. 1510-xxxxxx03 upon validation of the transfer of the City’s IRA to the designated AGDB account.

We recommended that the:
OCA to –
Strictly monitor and enforce the liquidation of CAs granted to AOs within the reglementary period and the immediate refund of unspent amounts;
Continue sending demand letters to all erring AOs who are still active in the service and to submit their names to the City Legal Officer for appropriate action including the immediate withholding of salaries and other unpaid claims due them; and
Require the immediate submission of the lacking documents from the AO concerned to facilitate evaluation on the request for write-off of cash advances not exceeding P100,000.00 as provided under COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016;
CLO to recommend to the City Mayor appropriate action to be taken for AOs who are already separated from the service; and
City Administrator to constantly remind concerned officials and employees on the pertinent laws, rules and regulations governing the handling of cash advance every time they are granted with the same.

We recommended that Management require the:
PCGH and DMLI to furnish the OCA with copies of the HCTL submitted to PhilHealth to provide basis for the recording in the books of the accrued income , receivables and liabilities;
CHO to take into consideration how to immediately resolve the issue regarding the poor internet connectivity at DMLI, and if no feasible short-term solution could be reached, to ensure that there is constant communication between DMLI and OCA; and
OCA to effect adjustments to present the correct balances of the affected accounts upon submission of the supporting documents.

We recommended that the OCA:
Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the balances of the affected accounts for fair presentation in the FS, and copy furnish the CAO for evaluation; and
Cease the practice of incorporating internal monitoring record of events which are not cognizable in the books of accounts pursuant to the recognition criteria laid down in IPSAS.

We recommended that Management:
Create a dedicated appraisal committee from the offices/departments concerned to –
Compute and determine the proper value of the removed/replaced costs of parts/materials that need to be derecognized in the books, and furnish CAO with a copy of the committee’s appraisal report;
Consider the use of cost of the replacement as allowed in paragraph 85 of IPSAS 17, should it be impracticable to determine the carrying amount of the replaced parts; and
Include as part of the standard operating procedure, the appraisal of costs of replaced/removed materials whenever there are repairs, renovations or rehabilitations made in any of the City’s buildings and structures; and
Require the OCA to post the journal entries, to derecognize in the books the cost of the removed/replaced parts based on the report of the appraisal committee.

We recommended that Management require close coordination of the OCA, CGSO and the OCE to establish procedures that would ensure proper documentation and reporting of demolition of buildings and structures, and submission to the OCA for recording in the books of accounts.

We also recommended that Management require the CGSO to ensure proper documentation of the demolished school buildings as basic of the OCA to derecognize the carrying amounts thereof.
We recommended that the Management require the CGSO and OCA to escalate their efforts to come up with reconciled details of their respective records supporting the School Buildings account.

We recommended that the Management require:
The GSO and OCA to reconcile their records based on appropriate documents to come up with harmonized reports;
The OCA to re-compute the depreciation expenses recorded and prepare adjusting entries, as warranted, to correct the misstatement in the affected accounts; and
The OCE to establish, as part of the process, the submission of the Certificates of Inspection and Acceptance to the OCA and GSO for an identical source document in the preparation of required reports.

We recommended that the OCA cleanse the books, complete the information required in the system to have the eNGAS facility operate effectively as designed and generate desired reports, and adjust any errors that may be discovered.
We recommended that the OCA effect the necessary adjustments to correct the misclassifications identified.

We recommended that the:
OCE to –
Ensure that the QRGPPAs is complete, accurate and reliable pursuant to Section 477 (b) (3) of RA No. 7160 which grants the City Engineer the authority to administer, coordinate, supervise, and control public works projects of the City;
Conduct the inspection and monitoring of all on-going projects notwithstanding the non-submission of progress billing and ARs by the contractors and complete the information in the QRGPPAs;
GSO report all properties counted during the physical count in the RPCPPE; and
OCA to –
Cause the reconciliation of the Schedule of PPE, RPCPPE and the QRGPPAs; and
Establish a process or mechanism which requires the OCE to submit a report of ongoing infrastructure projects  with values to enable it to recognize in the books the accomplished portions of the same and the corresponding payables.

We recommended that:
The PCSDO to –
Strictly observe prompt submission to the OCA of the claims for payment of expenses incurred at year end; and
Devise a control for the early submission of billing statements from various schools in order to facilitate the preparation of disbursement vouchers; and
The OCA to –
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances of the affected accounts for fair presentation in the FS; and
Strictly observe the accrual basis of accounting as provided under IPSAS 1 in the recognition of expenses incurred.
We recommended that the CTO continue to adopt effective collection strategies and sustain effective tax information campaign to consistently attain or surpass the targets set in revenue collection.

We recommended that:
The CTO conduct immediate inspection of the 2,175 business establishments. If warranted, require the payment of business taxes, licenses and payment of applicable fees for those that had retired or no longer in operation pursuant to Section 88 of the City Ordinance; and
The BPLO include, as part of its established procedures, the monitoring, analysis and investigation of business establishments that have neither applied for renewal of permit nor applied for business retirement.

We recommended that Management:
Require settlement of tax deficiencies, if any, on business establishments transacting with the City;
Initiate appropriate action against the concerned business establishments for the non-declaration of the correct gross receipts, pursuant to Section 37 of the Pasay City Revenue Code; and.
Require –
The OCT to:
Refrain from accepting payment for current accounts if the tax deficiency, including penalties and surcharges, found in audit are not settled;
Issue demand letters to the business establishments who are still in operation but not paying the correct taxes and fees to the City; and
Conduct its explicit function to examine the books of accounts of business establishments and issue Letter of Assessment indicating the results of its examination; and
The BPLO to deny the issuance of business permits to establishments unless the tax deficiencies are first settled.

We recommended that:
The OCA to comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-009; and
The CTO to push through with the enhancement of the system that would allow the presentation of breakdown of payment in accordance with the classification of business income collections.

We recommended that Management require the heads of departments/offices to:
Endeavor to learn from the past GPB preparation exercise and become steadfast in their responsibility to submit their respective GPBs to the GFPS - TWG on or before the date set for submission; and
Undergo GAD seminar-workshop to acquaint themselves with mainstreaming GADable PPAs and how these are attributed in their departments’/offices’ budgets.

We recommended that the City exert active efforts in mobilizing its proponent offices to accomplish their targeted and planned PPAs. We also recommended that the GFPS intensify its collaborative efforts with departments/offices concerned in carrying out the programmed activities.
We recommended that the City require the GFPS to conduct annual monitoring and evaluation of the gender-responsiveness of all the PPAs listed in the GPB for informed decision-making.

We recommended that the City appoint a representative from the private sector and reinstate the SK Federation President as members of the GFPS for a holistic identification of gender related issues and concerns.
The Head of the GFPS in her letter dated July 8, 2020 agreed to implement the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the CLO:
Request a copy of the Writ of Possession and Order of Expropriation, in all of its expropriation cases from the Clerk of Court to cloth the City with the rightful possession over the property; and
Keep a complete file of legal documents issued by the court and immediately seek, as a matter of right, all orders/documents they are entitled to.
We recommended that Management require the timely submission by the CHO of the quarterly report to PhilHealth and to the CAO - Pasay, as required under PhilHealth Circular No. 15.

We recommended that the OCE ensure that the public is notified of the required information of all ongoing PPAs it undertakes in accordance with the General Guidelines laid down in COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that the City, through its BAC, submit the required PMR, complete with the required information and with due regard to the prescribed timelines pursuant to Section 12.2 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184, or as clarified under GPPB Resolution No. 11-2020.

We recommended that the BAC strictly follow the procurement steps by including in its established process the Bid Evaluation activity to ensure that contracts are awarded to the rightful bidder with the Lowest Calculated and Responsive Bid or to the Single Calculated and Responsive Bid, if there was a lone qualified bidder, to the advantage of the City.
We recommended that the BAC strictly comply with the requirement of invitation in all stages of procurement to be able to achieve enhanced transparency in procurement as intended by the law.

We recommended that the OCA:
Remit the withheld taxes in full within the required period to avoid penalties that may be imposed by the BIR.  Otherwise, the responsible official shall be held liable for the payment of penalties resulting from the delay as provided under the NIRC of 1997; and
Revisit the existing procedures with the end in view of addressing the noted observations.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management intensify its investigation regarding the beginning balance of P997,841.36, not backed-up with actual cash on hand, and submit the report thereon for any subsequent appropriate action.
We further recommended that:
The OCA reverse the entries made in CY 2018 pertaining to the adjustments made with inadequate supporting documents; and
The City Treasurer’s Office (CTO) and the OCA work together, to exhaust all available records to identify the composition of Cash - Local Treasury account and prepare adjusting entries duly supported with relevant documents, if warranted, based on the result of the analysis.

We recommended that Management immediately transfer their deposits from PNB to any of the AGDBs listed under Section 2 of the DOF Memorandum Circular, with due consideration of Section 6 of the DOF DC No. 01-2017 providing that the Heads of LGUs shall be responsible with the compliance of the Circular without prejudice to criminal and/or administrative liability in accordance with existing laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management:
Discontinue recognizing the contingent asset in the FS, rather, disclose a brief description of its nature in the Notes to the Financial Statements, and where practicable, an estimate of its financial effect; and prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to effect the adjustment, copy furnish the Office of the City Auditor for evaluation;
Assess and monitor the contingent assets to continually ensure that developments are appropriately reflected in the Notes to FS. Recognize the asset and related income in the FS in the period when it has become virtually certain that an inflow of economic benefits or service potential will arise and the asset’s value can be measured reliably; and
Undertake the timely remittance of the 5% contribution directly to MMDA to avoid the claim that the DBM “over charges” deduction from the City’s IRA.

We recommended that Management:
Ensure that all insurable properties of the City are fully and adequately covered by property insurance pursuant to Section 5 of RA 656 and AO No. 4 series of 2017; and
Require the OCA to effect adjustment on the total insurance expense recorded in CY 2018 to recognize as asset the unexpired portion pursuant to the accrual method of accounting.

We recommended that Management:
Create a team tasked to determine the current FMV of the four parcels of land as required under IPSAS 23; and
Recognize in the books the four parcels of land reserved by the Republic in favor of the City pursuant to IPSAS 23.
We recommended that Management:
Create an Appraisal Committee from the City offices/departments concerned to compute and determine the proper value of the removed/replaced costs of parts/materials that need to be derecognized in the books or use the cost of the replacement as an indication of what the cost of the replaced part was at the time it was acquired or constructed;
Derecognize in the books, based on the report of the Appraisal Committee or the cost of the replacement, the costs of replaced/removed materials incident to the repairs, renovations and rehabilitations made; and
Include as part of standard operating procedure, the determination of cost of the replaced/removed materials whenever there are repairs, renovations or rehabilitations made in any of the City’s buildings and structures.
We recommended that Management:
Remind the personnel/s involve in the process of reclassification to place more attention in the proper transfer of long-term construction contracts to the appropriate PPE accounts when construction is completed and the structure/building is ready for its intended use; and
Prepare adjusting journal entries to transfer the completed projects from the CIP accounts to the appropriate PPE accounts.
We recommended that Management:
Devise a plan with the end purpose of completing the projects that were suspended/stopped;
Send notices to the contractors demanding the immediate completion of the projects, otherwise, forfeit the performance bonds posted by the respective contractors and venture into the possibility of rescinding the contract and award the same to a qualified contractor through negotiated contract pursuant to the IRR of RA No. 9184; and
Compute the amount of liquidated damages due from contractors concerned and deduct the same from the claims of the contractors.
We recommended that Management:
Dispose the unserviceable properties in accordance with the Manual on Disposal of Government Property and derecognize in the books once disposed; and
Commence the process of disposal for those unserviceable properties which were not yet covered with IIRUP as at year-end.

We recommended that the OCA:
Take up all the necessary adjusting entries to correct the overstatement and understatement of affected accounts to reflect the correct balances in the financial statements;
Analyze and confirm outstanding payables aged more than two years with no valid claims filed for possible reversion to the General Fund pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445; and
Ensure that all claims and recorded payables are duly supported with complete documents to establish the validity of the claims before recording the obligation pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 4 (s), Volume I of the Manual.

We recommended that Management require the OCA to:
Reconcile its records with the Philippine Reclamation Authority to substantiate the accuracy of the amount of claim against the City; and
Recognize in the books of accounts, if warranted, the obligation arising from the Compromise Agreement.

We recommended and the OCA agreed to:
Strictly observe the accrual basis of accounting as provided under IPSAS 1 in the recognition of expenses incurred;
Include in its process, a follow up procedure in the submission of transaction documents, clearly setting up a cut-off date in the submission of schools to DepEd – PCDO and the subsequent submission of DepEd – PCDO to the OCA; and
Strictly observe the application of the appropriate accounting standard in the recognition of assets under IPSAS 17.

We recommended that:
The CHO review the circulars and guidelines issued by PhilHealth for the proper utilization of the PFPR funds, which was made available as assistance to the City constituents; and
The OCA carefully scrutinize the disbursement documents submitted for recording to ensure correct charging of expenditures and/or capture of transactions in the appropriate account and fund.
We recommended, and the Management agreed, to ensure quarterly submission by the CHO of the required report to PhilHealth and to CAO, with or without reportable transactions, as required under PhilHealth Circular No. 15 to ensure that problems and issues could be detected and addressed at the soonest possible time, and to be able to formulate appropriate long term solutions based on informed judgment.

We recommended that Management:
Immediately stop collecting Other Taxes - Situs Tax from the contractors/suppliers;
Recompute appropriate business tax collections and initiate a plan on how to credit to the respective accounts of the contractors and suppliers, the corresponding amounts of Situs Tax deducted from them or apply this to any computed underpayments of business taxes; and
Endeavor to impose taxes allowed only under RA No. 7160 after an enabling Ordinance is enacted and approved by the Sangguniang Panlungsod and the City Mayor, respectively.

We recommended that the CBO:
Conduct regular inspection of ongoing constructions to check compliance with the building standards to assure safety in use or habitation of buildings and structures;
Communicate with the barangays on the issuance of barangay building clearance to ensure compliance in obtaining the necessary permit prior to construction and other related activities; and
Conduct investigation of properties with no permit and file appropriate charges as provided under Section 213 of the National Building Code.

We recommended the following:
The City refrain from the enforcement of NBCDO MC No. 03 series of 2016 until an appropriate Ordinance has been enacted; and
The SP enact the Ordinance on the new rates for fees and charges that include the building permit fees.

We recommended that the PCDC convenes as a collective body and perform its functions and activities as mandated in Section 109 (a) of RA No. 7160 to ensure that the approved and budgeted PPAs sufficiently covered the priorities of every sector concerned; and initiate strict compliance to the requirements of JMC No. 1 dated November 18, 2016.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Charge the amount paid for the expenses totaling P65,893,121.60 against the General Fund and transfer the same amount to the SEF;
Direct the City Accountant to ascertain that all payments made out of SEF are strictly for allowable expenses enumerated under Section 4 of  DepEd – DBM – DILG JMC No. 1, series of 2017; and
Require the PCLSB to ensure that only allowable expenses are included as items of expenditures in the preparation and formulation of the SEF budget by strictly observing the pertinent provisions of the DepEd - DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1.

We recommended that the City, through the SWMB, assist each barangay/cluster of barangays in providing or establishing an MRF that is designed in accordance with the requirements of RA No. 9003 to attain the maximized gain aimed to be achieved by the law.

We recommended that Management consider the designation of permanent members from the labor and employment, and nutrition sectors, to ensure complete and continuous representation in securing the rights and welfare of children as envisioned by RA No. 9344.

We recommended the following:
The SP revisit Ordinance No. 4547 s. 2011 with the end-in-view of amending it to conform to the Guidelines on the Sale of Bidding Documents under the IRR of RA No. 9184;
The BAC stop applying the rates imposed by the City and follow the rates prescribed in the IRR of RA No. 9184; and
The Management eliminate the possibility of any issues, including the imposition of excessive fees for the sale of bid documents, among others, that would preclude the wide participation of prospective bidders to ensure competitive bidding.

We recommended that Management:
Expedite and prioritize the amendment/revision of the GAD Code;
Adopt the IRR of the Code; and
Post the GAD Code and its IRR for publication and information dissemination.

We recommended and the Management agreed, that the GAD FPS ensure compliance with the deadlines set under the JMC, by closely coordinating with the various departments on the timelines given in the preparation and submission of the respective GPBs, for the timely and accurate capture of GAD PPAs in the AIP to ensure alignment and support to the programming done at the department level.

We recommended that:
The Management -
Exert active efforts in accomplishing their targeted and planned Organization-Focused PPAs;
Endeavor not just to sustain its compliance with the minimum budget requirement on GAD PPAs as provided for by law, but to increase identification of gender-responsive PPAs to be covered by the annual budget until such time that all major programs of the City are attributed to GAD as envisioned under the PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2012-01; and
The GFPS intensify its collaborative efforts with departments/offices concerned in carrying out the programmed activities.

We recommended that Management appoint representatives from the private sector and the SK Federation President as members of the GFPS for a holistic identification and responsive action on GAD issues and concerns within the City.

We recommended that Management:
Investigate the difference between the number of claimants per payroll against the recorded number of senior citizens per database/file;
Strictly monitor the release of benefits to the rightful claimants to avoid incurrence of loss/misuse of funds and investigate the release of birthday cash gift not claimed by the rightful senior citizen; and
Study the possibility of releasing the senior citizens’ benefits to their existing bank accounts, if any, thru an Automated Teller Machine to ensure that benefits are actually received by the rightful claimants.

We recommended the immediate appointment of personnel for the PDAO to carry out fully the functions inherent thereto pursuant to 
RA No. 10070, for comprehensive coverage and representation for the welfare of the PWDs.

We recommended that the OCA:
Remit the withheld taxes in full, within the required period to avoid penalties that may be imposed by the BIR. Otherwise, the responsible official shall be held liable for the payment of penalties resulting from the delay as provided under the National Internal Revenue Code of 1997; and
Revisit the existing procedures with the end in view of addressing the noted observations.

We recommended that:
The PCDRRM Office -
Proactively plan for the PCDRRM PPAs with due consideration and alignment with the PPAs formulated on a sectoral, regional or nationwide scale conducted by the National Government and other  stakeholders to ensure programming of funds to priority needs of the City;
Carefully identify priority projects according to the risk exposure and vulnerability of the locality and provides for realistic funding requirements on the identified PPAs; and
The PCDRRM Council performs its function of regular monitoring of the implementation of the projects and activities related to Disaster Preparedness Program.

We recommended the following:
The Local Finance Committee provide realistic targets in all revenue sources of the City based on historical data or actual collection trends as well as the nature of the revenue source to come up with an objective report of excess in collections over the revenue projections that manifests real collection efficiency; and
The CTO and the OCA coordinate on the appropriate classification of revenue sources for accurate recording in the books of accounts.

We recommended that Management (a) require the Office of the City Treasurer and the Office of the City Accountant to work together with all available records in their custody to identify and analyze the nature of this account and prepare adjusting entries, if necessary, duly supported with the result of the analysis; and (b) direct the OCA to make the necessary adjusting entries for the erroneous recording of collections and deposits.
The City Treasurer in his letter dated May 18, 2018 commented that “[t]he CTO always ensure that collections are deposited intact daily. Said amount in question was accordingly a forwarded year-end balance since Year 2000, prior to the assumption of duties by the current City Treasurer and City Accountant. Since CY 2015, CTO and OCA jointly and continuously exerted extra efforts to further investigate, review and trace back the accounts to resolve the long overdue issue of the aforecited account, but to no avail due to unavailability of the needed records.”
Adjustments of the erroneous recording of collections and deposits was effected under JEV No. 221-18-04-242 dated April 30, 2018.

We recommended that Management send a letter of request to the City of Makati, particularly to the City Accountant, copy furnished the Supervising Auditor – Makati City, to do the favor of requesting from PNB – Makati the following documents/information:
Copies of bank statements and bank reconciliation statements from CY 1993 until the closure of PNB Combo Account previously maintained by Pasay City for the then Municipality of Makati; and
Seek clarification and/or additional information with respect to the withdrawals amounting to P5,000,000.00 and P6,043,077.26 dated October 14, 2004 and January 21, 2005, respectively, from the PNB-Makati account, by securing debit memos and/or other supporting documents.
We also recommended that Management coordinate with Makati City and request that any documents to be provided by the PNB relative to the subject bank account be shared with Pasay City, for the appropriate action to be taken in the closure of the bank account that is still carried in the books of Pasay City.
The Accountant in her letter dated May 18, 2018 commented that “[i]n April 2018, the OCA sent a letter requesting for cooperation to the Hon. Mayor of the City of Makati in connection with the PNB account. As of date, no formal reply was received from the City of Makati.  However, based on the initial phone follow-up from the OCA of Makati, the OCA of Pasay was informed that the OCA of Makati is still waiting for the documents from PNB.
The City of Pasay settled the P25.571 million held in trust for Makati City, which is P12.191 million over the amount of P13.380 million confirmed as receivable by the Office of the City Accountant and the City Treasurer of Makati, with the fund source coming from other Trust Fund accounts.
Review and examination of records revealed that sometime in CY 2010, the COA Auditor’s Office of the City, sent a confirmation letter addressed to the then City Accountant of Makati, requesting for the counterpart receivable balance of the amount held in trust by Pasay City in favor of Makati City recorded under the account Due to LGU-Makati City in the Trust Fund books of Pasay City, as of December 31, 2009.
In the reply to the confirmation request, the then City Accountant of Makati, confirmed that the City of Makati has a receivable (counterpart payable in Pasay City books) amounting to P13,379,635.86 as of December 31, 2009.
Likewise, a letter dated February 15, 2010 of the then City Treasurer-Makati, addressed to the then City Treasurer of Pasay, informed the latter that based on their records, the Makati  City  has still accounts receivables representing transfer of Makati deposits from Pasay City in the amount of P13,379,635.86. The letter also requested for the immediate settlement of the same.
It is also worth mentioning that as of December 31, 2009, the balance of the Due to Makati account is P2,432,363.72 as appearing in the books of the City Trust Fund. And for the same date, it was likewise confirmed by the City Accountant and City Treasurer of Makati City that their Due From Pasay amounted only to P13,379,635.86. It can be readily ascertained that there is a great difference between the two balances, hence a reconciliation should have been made by both cities before any settlement has been made. In the absence of any reconciliation, there would be no clear evidence as to the amount payable and receivable between the two aforesaid cities.
It can be then clearly ascertained from the records of the City of Makati itself that as of December 31, 2009, the amount owed by the City representing the then deposits of Makati City amounts to P13,379,635.86 only.
Further examination of records also revealed that the bank account in question has deposits amounting to P10,921,645.72 as shown in the bank reconciliation statement prepared by the City of Makati as of December 31, 2003. Be it noted that in the same year, the account is already in the possession and control of Makati City, the preparation of the bank reconciliation statement is a proof of the same.
In sum, the Pasay City has a total of P24,301,281.58, payable to the City of Makati representing deposits of the same, arrived at as follows:
However, in June 2010, the City paid the City of Makati in the total amount of P25,570,772.95 under PNB Check No. 1465 amounting to P10,000,000.00 and PVB Check No. 65212 amounting to P15,570,772.95. The total amount of P25,570,772.95 represents the check dated February 23, 1996, which bounced due to insufficient funds and reported to have been found in the vault of Makati City and was demanded replacement by the City of Makati.
Granting that the amount payable to Makati City is indeed P25,570,772.95, analysis would reveal that the City have overpaid Makati City in the amount of P12,191,137.09, as computed below.
Moreover, the amount of P25,570,772.95 which was paid  in  two checks, PNB Check No. 1465 amounting to P10,000,000.00 and PVB Check No. 65212 amounting to P15,570,772.95, was sourced from other Trust Fund accounts. The  use of funds from other Trust Fund accounts resulted in unfunded portion of the Trust Liabilities to the extent of P25,570,772.92. The particular trust funds where the payment was sourced from cannot be determined with certainty even up to this date by the Office of City Accountant as well as the City Treasurer’s Office.
The use of the funds coming from these Trust Fund accounts is illegal, as this is contrary to the fundamental principles laid down under Sec. 309 of RA 7160, otherwise known as the Local Government Code, and further reiterated in the COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992, which states that:
“Trust Funds shall consist of private and public monies which have officially come into the possession of the local government or of a local government official as trustee, agent or administrator, or which have been received as a guaranty for the fulfillment of some obligation. A trust fund shall only be used for specific purpose for which it was created or for which it came into the possession of the local government unit.” Underscoring ours.
We recommended that Management continue to investigate the matter to establish the amount of overpayment made to the City Government of Makati and to work out the settlement of the same.

Ensure that the proposed PPAs are supported with thorough studies and plans, with due consideration of existing PPAs of other offices of the City, in order to establish their viability and implementation for the attainment of the objectives of the law; and

The City Accountant in her letter dated May 18, 2018 commented that “[t]he OCA shall comply with your audit recommendation to implement the accrual method, provided that the PCGH and Doña Marta Lying-In furnish the OCA with copies of the documents submitted to PhilHealth, including the billings, and provided further that PCGH, Doña Marta Lying-In and Philhealth give their assurance that the amount of reimbursement from Philhealth is equal to the amount of the billing sent to it. Otherwise, OCA cannot implement the accrual method as this will result in overstatement of accruals, considering that the amount billed is usually more than the amount actually reimbursed, since it is Philhealth that determines the amount to be reimbursed to PCGH and Doña Marta Lying-In.”
Auditor’s rejoinder:
Whatever internal arrangement or process or management control that will be instituted between the offices of the City, is a purely management function. Coordination on that matter will be done by management. The audit stand point in this issue is the proper accounting of transactions entered into by PCGH and Doña Marta Lying-In, which is to apply PPSAS that requires the application of accrual method in recording transactions, otherwise, the fairness of reported accounts and amounts in the financial statements will be affected.

observations.recommendation:
We  recommended that Management require:

The City Treasurer and the BPL Officer to exercise their mandated functions to examine the books of business establishments within the City’s territorial jurisdiction; and 



The BPLO to conduct regular ocular inspection of all establishments to identify those in operation and appropriate action be instituted against establishments conducting business without required permits from the City.


The Data Controller IV under CTO amends the declared gross sales/receipts of taxpayers without reference to credible evidences to support the amendment.

We recommended that Management require the City Treasurer to:

Ensure that authority is given only to the authorized personnel to amend the declared gross sales/receipts of the taxpayers and that such amendment is evaluated and approved by higher official and submission of  any legal basis to support such authority; and

Comply strictly with the policies and procedures of Republic Act No. 9485 known as the “Anti-Red Tape Act of 2007” and the Department Heads to continuously monitor their staff.

Payments for business taxes, mayor’s permit fees, community tax certificate and other fees and taxes were recorded twice in the Business Permits and Licenses System (BPLS) – one for the proper taxpayer and the other for another taxpayer, the latter’s being fraudulently updated with the payment made by the former.

Various suppliers and contractor of the City underdeclared their 2015 and 2016 gross sales upon renewal of their 2016 and 2017 license to operate, as a result, the City was deprived of P7.257 million in 2016 and P6.318 million in 2017, for a total of P13.575 million.

We recommended that Management require the City Treasurer to:

Issue a demand to the contractors identified in the annexes to pay their delinquent business permit fees inclusive of interest and surcharges, otherwise, issue an order cancelling their license to operate; and



Inspect the books of accounts of other business taxpayers in order to determine if the same misrepresentation is present in other taxpayers records aside from the samples taken.

The City could have generated additional funds for its programs and services to benefit its constituents had the Pasay Revenue Tax Code been updated.

We recommended that Management conduct an in-depth study of the probability to increase to the rates of business taxes. Likewise, coordinate with the Sanggunian Panlungsod for the passage of an ordinance relative to the subject matter.
observations.managementAction:
CTO religiously conducts the examination of the books of accounts especially those who fail to submit the required documents for verification purposes of their declared gross sales.

BPLO continuously conduct regular ocular inspection of all establishments to identify those in operation and appropriate action be instituted against establishments conducting business without required permits from the City.



The City Treasurer complied with the recommendation of the COA to ensure that authority is only given to the authorized personnel.



CTO strictly complied and continuously complies with the policies and procedures of RA  No. 9485 in monitoring staff.



Demand Letters were sent by the CTO to the identified taxpayers. Some Suppliers/ Contractors identified with tax deficiency already complied or paid their shortages. Some others sent letter justification with the COA, while the rest will be tagged during their renewal of business permits. 

CTO has inspected and continuously inspects the books of other business taxpayers as recommended by COA.



The City agreed to conduct a study of the probability to increase to the rates of business taxes.

On-going examination of books of taxpayers





BPLO issued citation slips and notices to the taxpayers with violation








Declaration of sales receipts/sales still approved by unauthorized personnel and not evaluated by proper authority









On-going inspection of books of account of business  taxpayers.





Revision of the Local Revenue Code still on process.

We recommended that the Local Finance Committee:

Carry out periodic financial performance analysis to be able to determine the City’s financial capability, to further improve its collection efficiency and what needs to be done through proper budget allocation,  and  to assess the adequacy of the existing internal control to determine the underlying causes of the weaknesses in the area of cash management;

Closely monitor liquidity condition to see if there is a need to make adjustments in the CGP’s financial operations by providing a realistic and achievable estimate based on the trend of revenue collections; and 

Adopt cost cutting measures and strategies to improve its cash position.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to require:

The CBO to set up the statutory lump-sum appropriation for GAD in the AEB with a breakdown of itemized GAD-related PPAs mainstreamed in various City offices that should be reconciled with the GADPB; and

The CPDO to coordinate with the CBO to see to it that the GAD budget in the AEB is reconciled with the GADPB.

GAD accomplishment for PPAs implemented disclosed a very low utilization rate equivalent to only 30.40 percent.

We recommended and Management agreed to require:

The GFPS to strictly monitor annually the implementation of PPAs in the GADPB in terms of its success, to further review the relevance of planned PPAs and the outcome of the budget for effective planning and gender-responsive implementation by giving priority to those PPAs that address emerging and/or continuing issues and concerns for the realization of the CGP’s commitments, plans and policies on GAD;

b. The City Treasurer to ensure that the PPAs in the GADPB is provided with adequate fund resources sufficient for their implementation; and

c. The City Accountant to monitor and account the expenditures incurred in the utilization of GAD budget and submit a report thereon to COA.

The formulation of GAD Code or ordinance was not prioritized.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management require the GFPS to expedite and prioritize  the formulation of  a GAD Code in the ensuing year for approval by the City Mayor and to be passed by the SP thru an ordinance to provide guidance in the planning, policy making and identification of GAD related PPAs.

GAD Database not linked with the Barangays GAD Database

We recommended that Management require the GFPS to coordinate the efforts of  the 201 Barangays and direct them to prepare and establish  their GAD Database for integration with the City’s GAD Database to ensure its relevance; to align with the City’s response to the gender issues and/or concerns within the locality and in the context of the City’s mandate; and to promote transparency and participatory governance.

138 Barangays without Violence Against Women and Children (VAWC) Desk

We reiterated our recommendation that Management strengthen and monitor the strict implementation on the establishment of VAWC Desk in all the 201 Barangays within the City to ensure its advocacy against VAWC.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor propose to the SP the assessment of the true status of the PCSC and for the enactment of an ordinance, supported with feasibility study, to lay the legal basis for its classification either as a profit-oriented entity as that of a LEE or merely as a social service-oriented entity.


The operation of PCSC resulted in successive losses over the years

We recommended that Management require the PCSC Head to:

Prepare and submit a 5-year business plan based on the outline provided in Appendix A of the above cited Manual; 



Formulate a rehabilitation program to improve the income of the PCSC including the improvement of its facilities;


Free use of the facilities and its utilization for other specific purpose not related to its operation.

We recommended that Management require the PCSC Head:

Enact ordinance that will clearly identify those who will be allowed to use the facilities free of charge; and

 Ensure that the utilization of the facility is in accordance with the objective and purpose of its establishment;


There are less users of the PCSC due to the poor condition of its facilities thus income generation is not maximized.

We recommended that Management require the PCSC Head to provide separate electric and water meters for the above mentioned four offices and charge their consumptions against their own budgets. However, in the case of Barangay 76 the PCSC should bill the former for the electricity and water consumption and impose rental fee for the spaces occupied by it.


The reported balances of the books and RPCPPE of the PCSC building of only P25.223 million are unreliable.

We recommended that Management direct the Chief Accountant and the General Services Officer to appraise the value of the PCSC building and Derham Park and record it separately in the books under the appropriate PPE accounts.

Costs attributable to the construction of swimming pool and renovation of PCSC were recorded as part of Cuneta Astrodome
